div.head: The Ambassador in Germany (Sackett) to the Acting Secretary of State
div.p:
109. The following statement has now been given to me:Quotation not paraphrased.
“In view of the fears which have sprung up in some circles that the amounts released in the German budget by the relief from reparation payments might be used to increase armaments, I declare that an increase in the appropriation for the army and navy during the holiday year has never been contemplated nor will it take place. The aggregate alleviations accruing to Germany from the Hoover Plan are required and will be used in their entirety to cover the deficits in revenue which are to be expected, to consolidate financial conditions, and to save German economic life”.

In delivering the above statement to me Von BülowUnder Secretary of State in the German Ministry for Foreign Affairs. again stressed the point that Bruening still hoped that publishing such a statement would not be necessary and that in all likelihood no one in Germany anticipated that the appropriations for the army and navy would be increased by the amount saved in the budget. A statement such as this would only make people suspicious as to what commitments Germany might have made under pressure. However, if it were thought necessary to publish the statement, Chancellor Bruening would still want to do it in as inconspicuous a manner as possible; that is to say, to insert this statement in a speech if the occasion arose to deliver one in the next few days, otherwise to issue it through the Wolff Telegraph Service as an official communiqué. It seems to me that this view, along with the nature of the statement itself, brings out sharply the state of mind of the Government and the Chancellor and their extreme hesitation about taking the step which you have requested. In consideration of all the above circumstances may I have your instructions?The statement was released to the press in Washington on July 5; see Department of State, Press Releases, July 11, 1931, p. 39.
